---

## 2023 Solar PV Real-Time Revenue Comparison: Abilene (West Hub) vs Houston (Houston Hub)

### Executive Summary

**Assumptions:** 100 MW nameplate, double-axis tracking, 10% system losses, zero curtailment, real-time (not day-ahead) ERCOT hub LMPs at 15-minute settlement, solar generation modeled at hourly resolution via Renewables.ninja.

| Metric | Abilene (West Hub) | Houston (Houston Hub) |
|---|---|---|
| **Annual Generation** | 219,720 MWh | 193,193 MWh |
| **Capacity Factor** | 25.1% | 22.1% |
| **Annual Real-Time Revenue** | **$20,705,581** | **$19,590,466** |
| **Capture Price** | $94.24/MWh | $101.40/MWh |
| **Time-Wtd Avg Hub LMP** | $49.45/MWh | $49.58/MWh |
| **Revenue per MW** | $207,056 | $195,905 |

**Bottom line:** The Abilene/West Hub site generates **$1.12M (+5.7%) more annual revenue** despite a 7.1% *lower* capture price, because its 13.7% generation advantage (driven by better solar resource) more than compensates. Abilene outperforms Houston on 215 of 365 days (58.9%).

---

### Sources

- **Solar generation profiles:** Renewables.ninja API via `get_solar_profile` (lat/lon: 32.45°N, 99.75°W for Abilene; 29.76°N, 95.37°W for Houston; 2023-01-01 to 2023-12-31; 100,000 kW; double-axis tracking; 10% system loss).
- **Real-time LMPs:** ERCOT LMP database (`ercot_db.ercot_lmp`), filtered to `zone_type='hub'`, `zone_name='west'` and `'houston'`, `is_day_ahead=false`, covering 35,040 15-minute intervals for 2023.

---

### Key Drivers: Generation vs Price

| Factor | Direction | Magnitude |
|---|---|---|
| Generation advantage (Abilene) | ▲ Benefit | +13.7% (+26,527 MWh) |
| Capture price disadvantage (Abilene) | ▼ Drag | –7.1% (–$7.16/MWh) |
| **Net Revenue Impact** | ▲ | **+5.7% (+$1.12M)** |

The implied marginal value of the extra 26,527 MWh from Abilene is $42.04/MWh — well below the average capture price, reflecting that incremental solar generation occurs during mid-day hours when solar-driven price depression is strongest.

---

### Monthly Breakdown

| Month | Abilene Revenue | Houston Revenue | Difference | Abilene Gen (MWh) | Houston Gen (MWh) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Jan | $361,936 | $394,971 | –$33,036 | 16,385 | 14,253 |
| Feb | $245,952 | $268,839 | –$22,886 | 14,809 | 14,071 |
| Mar | $632,304 | $596,845 | +$35,459 | 19,001 | 16,135 |
| Apr | $461,433 | $432,760 | +$28,673 | 20,842 | 15,870 |
| May | $720,025 | $654,447 | +$65,578 | 16,213 | 15,231 |
| **Jun** | **$2,421,042** | **$2,131,852** | **+$289,190** | 20,017 | 17,902 |
| Jul | $1,521,524 | $1,471,158 | +$50,366 | 21,922 | 19,108 |
| **Aug** | **$8,870,617** | **$8,748,467** | **+$122,149** | 21,661 | 18,642 |
| Sep | $3,576,581 | $3,352,493 | +$224,088 | 18,317 | 16,988 |
| Oct | $707,795 | $688,342 | +$19,453 | 16,811 | 16,435 |
| Nov | $656,974 | $494,414 | +$162,560 | 16,568 | 13,541 |
| Dec | $529,398 | $355,877 | +$173,521 | 17,173 | 15,017 |

**August alone accounts for 43% of annual revenue at both sites**, driven by extreme heat-driven price spikes (average LMPs of ~$196–$199/MWh at both hubs). June and September are the next-largest months. Houston outperforms Abilene only in January and February, when generation is low and the slight Houston price premium matters more.

---

### Seasonal Summary

| Season | Abilene Revenue | Houston Revenue | Difference |
|---|---|---|---|
| Winter (DJF) | $1,137,286 | $1,019,687 | +$117,599 |
| Spring (MAM) | $1,813,762 | $1,684,052 | +$129,711 |
| **Summer (JJA)** | **$12,813,183** | **$12,351,478** | **+$461,705** |
| Fall (SON) | $4,941,350 | $4,535,249 | +$406,101 |

Summer dominates but Abilene leads in every season — the generation advantage is persistent year-round.

---

### Solar-Hour Price Dynamics

| Metric (during solar-producing hours) | Abilene/West | Houston/Houston |
|---|---|---|
| Solar-producing hours (of 8,760) | 4,467 | 4,409 |
| Time-weighted avg LMP | $75.77 | $78.05 |
| Generation-weighted avg LMP (capture) | $94.24 | $101.40 |
| Median LMP | $25.34 | $25.20 |
| 5th percentile LMP | –$1.82 | $11.10 |
| 95th percentile LMP | $150.72 | $146.94 |

Houston's solar-hour LMPs are moderately higher at the mean, but notably, **West Hub solar production faces substantially more negative-price exposure**: the 5th percentile LMP is –$1.82 vs +$11.10 at Houston. The capture prices ($94 vs $101) are well above time-weighted averages ($76–$78) because generation is concentrated in higher-priced afternoon hours, creating a "solar value premium" above the simple average.

---

### Hub Price Spread During Simultaneous Solar

When both sites are generating simultaneously (4,290 hours):
- **Mean spread (West – Houston): –$1.55/MWh** (West is slightly cheaper)
- **Median spread: $0.00/MWh** (identical half the time)
- **West > Houston: 42.7% of hours**

The hubs are generally well-coupled during solar hours, with a small Houston premium on average. This modest price differential explains why the capture price gap is only ~$7/MWh.

---

### Negative Price Exposure

| Metric | West Hub | Houston Hub |
|---|---|---|
| % of 15-min intervals with LMP < 0 | 8.6% | 1.4% |
| Revenue lost to negative prices | –$72,499 | –$2,606 |

West Hub has **~6× more negative price intervals** and **~28× more revenue erosion** from negative prices. This reflects West Texas's high wind and solar penetration, which more frequently pushes LMPs below zero. However, at ~$72K (0.35% of total revenue), the absolute impact remains modest.

---

### Top 5 Revenue Days

| Rank | Abilene (West Hub) | Revenue | | Houston (Houston Hub) | Revenue |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | June 20, 2023 | $1,535,582 | | Aug 17, 2023 | $1,471,980 |
| 2 | Aug 17, 2023 | $1,491,423 | | June 20, 2023 | $1,277,213 |
| 3 | Sep 6, 2023 | $1,252,590 | | Sep 6, 2023 | $1,205,659 |
| 4 | Aug 25, 2023 | $967,546 | | Aug 25, 2023 | $919,671 |
| 5 | Aug 30, 2023 | $803,705 | | Aug 10, 2023 | $860,036 |

The same scarcity events drive top days at both sites. June 20, 2023 (a day of extreme ERCOT tight conditions) was the single best day for Abilene, generating $1.54M from just 761 MWh — an implied capture price of ~$2,018/MWh for that day.

---

### Conclusion

For a 100 MW double-axis tracking solar farm operating in 2023 ERCOT real-time markets:

- **Abilene (West Hub): ~$20.7M/year** — superior insolation (25.1% CF) outweighs slightly lower hub prices and greater negative-price exposure.
- **Houston (Houston Hub): ~$19.6M/year** — ~$101/MWh capture price advantage but 22.1% CF limits total output.

The $1.1M differential (~$11,150/MW) favors West Texas siting purely on real-time energy revenue. However, the 7% capture price discount at West Hub suggests that as more solar builds in West Texas, the value erosion from solar cannibalization could deepen, potentially narrowing this advantage over time.

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Load solar profiles
abilene_solar = pd.read_csv('/work/solar_profile_20260613_112527.csv')
houston_solar = pd.read_csv('/work/solar_profile_20260613_112530.csv')

# Load ERCOT prices
west_prices = pd.read_csv('/work/ercot_db_20260613_112609.csv')
houston_prices = pd.read_csv('/work/ercot_db_20260613_112613.csv')

# Parse solar
def parse_solar(df):
    df['electricity_kw'] = df['value'].apply(lambda x: float(eval(x)['electricity']))
    df['datetime'] = pd.to_datetime(df['timestamp'], unit='ms')
    df = df.set_index('datetime')[['electricity_kw']]
    return df

abilene_solar_df = parse_solar(abilene_solar)
houston_solar_df = parse_solar(houston_solar)

# Parse prices
def parse_prices(df):
    df['datetime'] = pd.to_datetime(df['timestamp'])
    df = df.set_index('datetime')[['lmp']]
    return df

west_prices_df = parse_prices(west_prices)
houston_prices_df = parse_prices(houston_prices)

# For each 15-min price interval, map to the hourly solar value
# Create an 'hour' column for merging
west_prices_df['hour'] = west_prices_df.index.floor('H')
houston_prices_df['hour'] = houston_prices_df.index.floor('H')

# Merge: each 15-min price row gets the hourly solar output
abilene_merged = west_prices_df.join(abilene_solar_df, on='hour', how='left')
houston_merged = houston_prices_df.join(houston_solar_df, on='hour', how='left')

print("Abilene merge shape:", abilene_merged.shape)
print("Houston merge shape:", houston_merged.shape)

# Revenue per 15-min interval:
# Energy (MWh) = electricity_kW * 0.25h / 1000
# Revenue ($) = Energy (MWh) * LMP ($/MWh)
abilene_merged['revenue'] = abilene_merged['electricity_kw'] * 0.25 / 1000 * abilene_merged['lmp']
houston_merged['revenue'] = houston_merged['electricity_kw'] * 0.25 / 1000 * houston_merged['lmp']

# Annual totals
total_rev_abilene = abilene_merged['revenue'].sum()
total_rev_houston = houston_merged['revenue'].sum()

# Also compute MWh generation
total_mwh_abilene = abilene_merged['electricity_kw'].sum() * 0.25 / 1000
total_mwh_houston = houston_merged['electricity_kw'].sum() * 0.25 / 1000

# Average capture price
avg_capture_abilene = total_rev_abilene / total_mwh_abilene if total_mwh_abilene > 0 else 0
avg_capture_houston = total_rev_houston / total_mwh_houston if total_mwh_houston > 0 else 0

# Average LMPs (time-weighted)
avg_lmp_west = west_prices_df['lmp'].mean()
avg_lmp_houston = houston_prices_df['lmp'].mean()

# Monthly breakdown
abilene_merged['month'] = abilene_merged.index.month
houston_merged['month'] = houston_merged.index.month

monthly_abilene = abilene_merged.groupby('month').agg(
    revenue=('revenue', 'sum'),
    generation_mwh=('electricity_kw', lambda x: x.sum() * 0.25 / 1000),
    avg_lmp=('lmp', 'mean')
).reset_index()

monthly_houston = houston_merged.groupby('month').agg(
    revenue=('revenue', 'sum'),
    generation_mwh=('electricity_kw', lambda x: x.sum() * 0.25 / 1000),
    avg_lmp=('lmp', 'mean')
).reset_index()

# Seasonal stats
abilene_merged['season'] = abilene_merged.index.month.map({12:'Winter',1:'Winter',2:'Winter',3:'Spring',4:'Spring',5:'Spring',6:'Summer',7:'Summer',8:'Summer',9:'Fall',10:'Fall',11:'Fall'})
houston_merged['season'] = houston_merged.index.month.map({12:'Winter',1:'Winter',2:'Winter',3:'Spring',4:'Spring',5:'Spring',6:'Summer',7:'Summer',8:'Summer',9:'Fall',10:'Fall',11:'Fall'})

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# --- LOAD DATA ---
abilene_solar = pd.read_csv('/work/solar_profile_20260613_112527.csv')
houston_solar = pd.read_csv('/work/solar_profile_20260613_112530.csv')
west_prices = pd.read_csv('/work/ercot_db_20260613_112609.csv')
houston_prices = pd.read_csv('/work/ercot_db_20260613_112613.csv')

# --- PARSE ---
def parse_solar(df):
    df['electricity_kw'] = df['value'].apply(lambda x: float(eval(x)['electricity']))
    df['datetime'] = pd.to_datetime(df['timestamp'], unit='ms')
    df = df.set_index('datetime')[['electricity_kw']]
    return df

def parse_prices(df):
    df['datetime'] = pd.to_datetime(df['timestamp'])
    df = df.set_index('datetime')[['lmp']]
    return df

abilene_solar_df = parse_solar(abilene_solar)
houston_solar_df = parse_solar(houston_solar)
west_prices_df = parse_prices(west_prices)
houston_prices_df = parse_prices(houston_prices)

# --- MERGE: 15-min prices with hourly solar ---
west_prices_df['hour'] = west_prices_df.index.floor('h')
houston_prices_df['hour'] = houston_prices_df.index.floor('h')

abilene_merged = west_prices_df.join(abilene_solar_df, on='hour', how='left')
houston_merged = houston_prices_df.join(houston_solar_df, on='hour', how='left')

# Revenue: kW * 0.25h / 1000 * $/MWh
abilene_merged['revenue'] = abilene_merged['electricity_kw'] * 0.25 / 1000 * abilene_merged['lmp']
houston_merged['revenue'] = houston_merged['electricity_kw'] * 0.25 / 1000 * houston_merged['lmp']

# --- KEY METRICS ---
total_rev_abilene = abilene_merged['revenue'].sum()
total_rev_houston = houston_merged['revenue'].sum()
total_mwh_abilene = abilene_merged['electricity_kw'].sum() * 0.25 / 1000
total_mwh_houston = houston_merged['electricity_kw'].sum() * 0.25 / 1000
cap_abilene = total_rev_abilene / total_mwh_abilene
cap_houston = total_rev_houston / total_mwh_houston
cf_abilene = total_mwh_abilene / (100 * 8760)
cf_houston = total_mwh_houston / (100 * 8760)

print("=" * 75)
print("2023 SOLAR PV REAL-TIME REVENUE: ABILENE vs HOUSTON")
print("100 MW | Double-Axis Tracking | 10% System Loss | No Curtailment")
print("=" * 75)
print(f"\n{'Metric':<42} {'Abilene (West Hub)':>18} {'Houston (Houston Hub)':>20}")
print("-" * 80)
print(f"{'Annual Generation (MWh)':<42} {total_mwh_abilene:>18,.0f} {total_mwh_houston:>20,.0f}")
print(f"{'Capacity Factor':<42} {cf_abilene:>18.2%} {cf_houston:>20.2%}")
print(f"{'Annual Real-Time Revenue ($)':<42} {total_rev_abilene:>18,.0f} {total_rev_houston:>20,.0f}")
print(f"{'Capture Price ($/MWh)':<42} {cap_abilene:>18.2f} {cap_houston:>20.2f}")
print(f"{'Time-Wtd Avg Hub LMP ($/MWh)':<42} {west_prices_df['lmp'].mean():>18.2f} {houston_prices_df['lmp'].mean():>20.2f}")
print(f"{'Revenue per MW ($/MW)':<42} {total_rev_abilene/100:>18,.0f} {total_rev_houston/100:>20,.0f}")

# --- DECOMPOSITION ---
gen_adv = (total_mwh_abilene / total_mwh_houston - 1) * 100
price_disadv = (1 - cap_abilene / cap_houston) * 100
extra_mwh = total_mwh_abilene - total_mwh_houston
extra_rev = total_rev_abilene - total_rev_houston

print(f"\n{'DECOMPOSITION OF REVENUE DIFFERENCE':-^75}")
print(f"  Abilene generates {gen_adv:.1f}% MORE energy (+{extra_mwh:,.0f} MWh)")
print(f"  Abilene capture price is {price_disadv:.1f}% LOWER (${cap_abilene:.2f} vs ${cap_houston:.2f}/MWh)")
print(f"  Net: Abilene revenue is {(total_rev_abilene/total_rev_houston - 1)*100:.1f}% higher (+${extra_rev:,.0f})")
print(f"  Generation advantage outweighs price disadvantage")
print(f"  Implied marginal value of extra MWh: ${extra_rev/extra_mwh:.2f}/MWh")

# --- MONTHLY ---
abilene_merged['month'] = abilene_merged.index.month
houston_merged['month'] = houston_merged.index.month

ma = abilene_merged.groupby('month').agg(rev=('revenue','sum'), gen=('electricity_kw',lambda x:x.sum()*0.25/1000), lmp=('lmp','mean'))
mh = houston_merged.groupby('month').agg(rev=('revenue','sum'), gen=('electricity_kw',lambda x:x.sum()*0.25/1000), lmp=('lmp','mean'))

months = ['Jan','Feb','Mar','Apr','May','Jun','Jul','Aug','Sep','Oct','Nov','Dec']
print(f"\n{'MONTHLY BREAKDOWN':-^105}")
print(f"{'Mo':<4} {'Abilene Rev':>14} {'Houston Rev':>14} {'Diff':>12} {'Abi Gen MWh':>12} {'Hou Gen MWh':>12} {'W LMP':>8} {'H LMP':>8}")
print("-" * 105)
for i in range(12):
    a, h = ma.iloc[i], mh.iloc[i]
    print(f"{months[i]:<4} {a['rev']:>14,.0f} {h['rev']:>14,.0f} {a['rev']-h['rev']:>12,.0f} {a['gen']:>12,.0f} {h['gen']:>12,.0f} {a['lmp']:>8.2f} {h['lmp']:>8.2f}")

# --- SEASONAL ---
abilene_merged['season'] = abilene_merged.index.month.map({12:'Win',1:'Win',2:'Win',3:'Spr',4:'Spr',5:'Spr',6:'Sum',7:'Sum',8:'Sum',9:'Fal',10:'Fal',11:'Fal'})
houston_merged['season'] = houston_merged.index.month.map({12:'Win',1:'Win',2:'Win',3:'Spr',4:'Spr',5:'Spr',6:'Sum',7:'Sum',8:'Sum',9:'Fal',10:'Fal',11:'Fal'})

sa = abilene_merged.groupby('season').agg(rev=('revenue','sum'), gen=('electricity_kw',lambda x:x.sum()*0.25/1000))
sh = houston_merged.groupby('season').agg(rev=('revenue','sum'), gen=('electricity_kw',lambda x:x.sum()*0.25/1000))

print(f"\n{'SEASONAL':-^75}")
print(f"{'Season':<8} {'Abilene Rev':>14} {'Houston Rev':>14} {'Diff':>12} {'Abi Gen MWh':>12} {'Hou Gen MWh':>12}")
print("-" * 75)
for s in ['Win','Spr','Sum','Fal']:
    print(f"{s:<8} {sa.loc[s,'rev']:>14,.0f} {sh.loc[s,'rev']:>14,.0f} {sa.loc[s,'rev']-sh.loc[s,'rev']:>12,.0f} {sa.loc[s,'gen']:>12,.0f} {sh.loc[s,'gen']:>12,.0f}")

# --- SOLAR-HOUR PRICE STATS ---
hourly_ab = abilene_merged.groupby(abilene_merged.index.floor('h')).agg(lmp=('lmp','mean'), ekw=('electricity_kw','first'))
hourly_ho = houston_merged.groupby(houston_merged.index.floor('h')).agg(lmp=('lmp','mean'), ekw=('electricity_kw','first'))
sol_ab = hourly_ab[hourly_ab['ekw'] > 0]
sol_ho = hourly_ho[hourly_ho['ekw'] > 0]

print(f"\n{'PRICE STATS DURING SOLAR-PRODUCING HOURS':-^75}")
print(f"{'':<30} {'Abilene/West':>18} {'Houston/Houston':>18}")
print("-" * 66)
print(f"{'Solar hours (of 8760)':<30} {len(sol_ab):>18} {len(sol_ho):>18}")
print(f"{'Time-wtd avg LMP ($/MWh)':<30} {sol_ab['lmp'].mean():>18.2f} {sol_ho['lmp'].mean():>18.2f}")
print(f"{'Gen-wtd avg LMP ($/MWh)':<30} {np.average(sol_ab['lmp'],weights=sol_ab['ekw']):>18.2f} {np.average(sol_ho['lmp'],weights=sol_ho['ekw']):>18.2f}")
print(f"{'Median LMP ($/MWh)':<30} {sol_ab['lmp'].median():>18.2f} {sol_ho['lmp'].median():>18.2f}")
print(f"{'5th percentile LMP ($/MWh)':<30} {sol_ab['lmp'].quantile(0.05):>18.2f} {sol_ho['lmp'].quantile(0.05):>18.2f}")
print(f"{'95th percentile LMP ($/MWh)':<30} {sol_ab['lmp'].quantile(0.95):>18.2f} {sol_ho['lmp'].quantile(0.95):>18.2f}")

# --- HUB SPREAD DURING SIMULTANEOUS SOLAR ---
h_comp = hourly_ab[['lmp','ekw']].join(hourly_ho[['lmp','ekw']], lsuffix='_w', rsuffix='_h')
both = h_comp[(h_comp['ekw_w']>0) & (h_comp['ekw_h']>0)]
both['spread'] = both['lmp_w'] - both['lmp_h']
print(f"\n{'HUB SPREAD (West - Houston) DURING SIMULTANEOUS SOLAR':-^75}")
print(f"  Hours with solar at both: {len(both)}")
print(f"  Mean spread: ${both['spread'].mean():.2f}/MWh (West cheaper by $1.55)")
print(f"  Median spread: ${both['spread'].median():.2f}/MWh")
print(f"  West > Houston: {(both['spread']>0).mean()*100:.1f}% of hours")

# --- NEGATIVE PRICES ---
neg_w = (west_prices_df['lmp'] < 0).mean() * 100
neg_h = (houston_prices_df['lmp'] < 0).mean() * 100
neg_rev_w = abilene_merged[abilene_merged['lmp'] < 0]['revenue'].sum()
neg_rev_h = houston_merged[houston_merged['lmp'] < 0]['revenue'].sum()
print(f"\n{'NEGATIVE PRICE EXPOSURE':-^75}")
print(f"  % of 15-min intervals with LMP < 0: West={neg_w:.1f}%, Houston={neg_h:.1f}%")
print(f"  Revenue lost to negative prices: West=${neg_rev_w:,.0f}, Houston=${neg_rev_h:,.0f}")
print(f"  West Hub has ~6x more negative price intervals and ~28x more revenue loss")

# --- TOP DAYS ---
daily_a = abilene_merged.groupby(abilene_merged.index.date).agg(rev=('revenue','sum'), gen=('electricity_kw',lambda x:x.sum()*0.25/1000))
daily_h = houston_merged.groupby(houston_merged.index.date).agg(rev=('revenue','sum'), gen=('electricity_kw',lambda x:x.sum()*0.25/1000))

print(f"\n{'TOP 5 REVENUE DAYS':-^75}")
print(f"{'Rank':<6} {'Abilene Date':<14} {'Rev ($)':>14} {'MWh':>10}  |  {'Houston Date':<14} {'Rev ($)':>14} {'MWh':>10}")
print("-" * 85)
top_a = daily_a.nlargest(5, 'rev')
top_h = daily_h.nlargest(5, 'rev')
for i in range(5):
    da = top_a.index[i]; dh = top_h.index[i]
    print(f"{i+1:<6} {str(da):<14} {top_a.loc[da,'rev']:>14,.0f} {top_a.loc[da,'gen']:>10,.0f}  |  {str(dh):<14} {top_h.loc[dh,'rev']:>14,.0f} {top_h.loc[dh,'gen']:>10,.0f}")

# --- DAY-COUNT ---
md = daily_a.join(daily_h, lsuffix='_a', rsuffix='_h')
ab_wins = (md['rev_a'] > md['rev_h']).sum()
print(f"\n{'DAILY COMPARISON':-^75}")
print(f"  Abilene > Houston: {ab_wins} of {len(md)} days ({ab_wins/len(md)*100:.1f}%)")
print(f"  Houston > Abilene: {len(md)-ab_wins} of {len(md)} days ({(len(md)-ab_wins)/len(md)*100:.1f}%)")

steps.content:
---

## 2023 Solar PV Real-Time Revenue Comparison: Abilene (West Hub) vs Houston (Houston Hub)

### Executive Summary

**Assumptions:** 100 MW nameplate, double-axis tracking, 10% system losses, zero curtailment, real-time (not day-ahead) ERCOT hub LMPs at 15-minute settlement, solar generation modeled at hourly resolution via Renewables.ninja.

| Metric | Abilene (West Hub) | Houston (Houston Hub) |
|---|---|---|
| **Annual Generation** | 219,720 MWh | 193,193 MWh |
| **Capacity Factor** | 25.1% | 22.1% |
| **Annual Real-Time Revenue** | **$20,705,581** | **$19,590,466** |
| **Capture Price** | $94.24/MWh | $101.40/MWh |
| **Time-Wtd Avg Hub LMP** | $49.45/MWh | $49.58/MWh |
| **Revenue per MW** | $207,056 | $195,905 |

**Bottom line:** The Abilene/West Hub site generates **$1.12M (+5.7%) more annual revenue** despite a 7.1% *lower* capture price, because its 13.7% generation advantage (driven by better solar resource) more than compensates. Abilene outperforms Houston on 215 of 365 days (58.9%).

---

### Sources

- **Solar generation profiles:** Renewables.ninja API via `get_solar_profile` (lat/lon: 32.45°N, 99.75°W for Abilene; 29.76°N, 95.37°W for Houston; 2023-01-01 to 2023-12-31; 100,000 kW; double-axis tracking; 10% system loss).
- **Real-time LMPs:** ERCOT LMP database (`ercot_db.ercot_lmp`), filtered to `zone_type='hub'`, `zone_name='west'` and `'houston'`, `is_day_ahead=false`, covering 35,040 15-minute intervals for 2023.

---

### Key Drivers: Generation vs Price

| Factor | Direction | Magnitude |
|---|---|---|
| Generation advantage (Abilene) | ▲ Benefit | +13.7% (+26,527 MWh) |
| Capture price disadvantage (Abilene) | ▼ Drag | –7.1% (–$7.16/MWh) |
| **Net Revenue Impact** | ▲ | **+5.7% (+$1.12M)** |

The implied marginal value of the extra 26,527 MWh from Abilene is $42.04/MWh — well below the average capture price, reflecting that incremental solar generation occurs during mid-day hours when solar-driven price depression is strongest.

---

### Monthly Breakdown

| Month | Abilene Revenue | Houston Revenue | Difference | Abilene Gen (MWh) | Houston Gen (MWh) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Jan | $361,936 | $394,971 | –$33,036 | 16,385 | 14,253 |
| Feb | $245,952 | $268,839 | –$22,886 | 14,809 | 14,071 |
| Mar | $632,304 | $596,845 | +$35,459 | 19,001 | 16,135 |
| Apr | $461,433 | $432,760 | +$28,673 | 20,842 | 15,870 |
| May | $720,025 | $654,447 | +$65,578 | 16,213 | 15,231 |
| **Jun** | **$2,421,042** | **$2,131,852** | **+$289,190** | 20,017 | 17,902 |
| Jul | $1,521,524 | $1,471,158 | +$50,366 | 21,922 | 19,108 |
| **Aug** | **$8,870,617** | **$8,748,467** | **+$122,149** | 21,661 | 18,642 |
| Sep | $3,576,581 | $3,352,493 | +$224,088 | 18,317 | 16,988 |
| Oct | $707,795 | $688,342 | +$19,453 | 16,811 | 16,435 |
| Nov | $656,974 | $494,414 | +$162,560 | 16,568 | 13,541 |
| Dec | $529,398 | $355,877 | +$173,521 | 17,173 | 15,017 |

**August alone accounts for 43% of annual revenue at both sites**, driven by extreme heat-driven price spikes (average LMPs of ~$196–$199/MWh at both hubs). June and September are the next-largest months. Houston outperforms Abilene only in January and February, when generation is low and the slight Houston price premium matters more.

---

### Seasonal Summary

| Season | Abilene Revenue | Houston Revenue | Difference |
|---|---|---|---|
| Winter (DJF) | $1,137,286 | $1,019,687 | +$117,599 |
| Spring (MAM) | $1,813,762 | $1,684,052 | +$129,711 |
| **Summer (JJA)** | **$12,813,183** | **$12,351,478** | **+$461,705** |
| Fall (SON) | $4,941,350 | $4,535,249 | +$406,101 |

Summer dominates but Abilene leads in every season — the generation advantage is persistent year-round.

---

### Solar-Hour Price Dynamics

| Metric (during solar-producing hours) | Abilene/West | Houston/Houston |
|---|---|---|
| Solar-producing hours (of 8,760) | 4,467 | 4,409 |
| Time-weighted avg LMP | $75.77 | $78.05 |
| Generation-weighted avg LMP (capture) | $94.24 | $101.40 |
| Median LMP | $25.34 | $25.20 |
| 5th percentile LMP | –$1.82 | $11.10 |
| 95th percentile LMP | $150.72 | $146.94 |

Houston's solar-hour LMPs are moderately higher at the mean, but notably, **West Hub solar production faces substantially more negative-price exposure**: the 5th percentile LMP is –$1.82 vs +$11.10 at Houston. The capture prices ($94 vs $101) are well above time-weighted averages ($76–$78) because generation is concentrated in higher-priced afternoon hours, creating a "solar value premium" above the simple average.

---

### Hub Price Spread During Simultaneous Solar

When both sites are generating simultaneously (4,290 hours):
- **Mean spread (West – Houston): –$1.55/MWh** (West is slightly cheaper)
- **Median spread: $0.00/MWh** (identical half the time)
- **West > Houston: 42.7% of hours**

The hubs are generally well-coupled during solar hours, with a small Houston premium on average. This modest price differential explains why the capture price gap is only ~$7/MWh.

---

### Negative Price Exposure

| Metric | West Hub | Houston Hub |
|---|---|---|
| % of 15-min intervals with LMP < 0 | 8.6% | 1.4% |
| Revenue lost to negative prices | –$72,499 | –$2,606 |

West Hub has **~6× more negative price intervals** and **~28× more revenue erosion** from negative prices. This reflects West Texas's high wind and solar penetration, which more frequently pushes LMPs below zero. However, at ~$72K (0.35% of total revenue), the absolute impact remains modest.

---

### Top 5 Revenue Days

| Rank | Abilene (West Hub) | Revenue | | Houston (Houston Hub) | Revenue |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | June 20, 2023 | $1,535,582 | | Aug 17, 2023 | $1,471,980 |
| 2 | Aug 17, 2023 | $1,491,423 | | June 20, 2023 | $1,277,213 |
| 3 | Sep 6, 2023 | $1,252,590 | | Sep 6, 2023 | $1,205,659 |
| 4 | Aug 25, 2023 | $967,546 | | Aug 25, 2023 | $919,671 |
| 5 | Aug 30, 2023 | $803,705 | | Aug 10, 2023 | $860,036 |

The same scarcity events drive top days at both sites. June 20, 2023 (a day of extreme ERCOT tight conditions) was the single best day for Abilene, generating $1.54M from just 761 MWh — an implied capture price of ~$2,018/MWh for that day.

---

### Conclusion

For a 100 MW double-axis tracking solar farm operating in 2023 ERCOT real-time markets:

- **Abilene (West Hub): ~$20.7M/year** — superior insolation (25.1% CF) outweighs slightly lower hub prices and greater negative-price exposure.
- **Houston (Houston Hub): ~$19.6M/year** — ~$101/MWh capture price advantage but 22.1% CF limits total output.

The $1.1M differential (~$11,150/MW) favors West Texas siting purely on real-time energy revenue. However, the 7% capture price discount at West Hub suggests that as more solar builds in West Texas, the value erosion from solar cannibalization could deepen, potentially narrowing this advantage over time.
